essay: A hundred leagues of ocean lay between the search party and Lanka, and only one among them could cross it. Hanuman (हनुमान) leapt. That leap defines him. Son of the wind-god Vayu and the apsara Anjana, he is the vanara who embodies strength wedded to surrender. The Ramayana presents him as Rama's greatest devotee, and later tradition counts him among the chiranjivi, the immortals who remain present in the world wherever the name of Rama is sung.

Hanuman's deeds are narrated chiefly in Valmiki's Ramayana, above all the Sundara Kanda, which recounts his crossing to Lanka, his discovery of Sita in the Ashoka grove, and the burning of the city. The Mahabharata's Vana Parva records his meeting with Bhima, his half-brother through Vayu. For most North Indian devotees the living text is Tulsidas's Ramcharitmanas, composed in Awadhi in the sixteenth century, and his Hanuman Chalisa. The Skanda Purana and Brahmanda Purana preserve further hymns and the eleventh Rudra identification.

Hanuman is shown with a monkey's face and a powerful human frame, often a vivid vermilion (sindoor) coat applied by devotees as offering. He carries the gada (mace) in one hand and frequently a mountain in the other, recalling the Sundara Kanda episode where he bore the Dronagiri peak bearing the sanjivani herb to revive Lakshmana. His tail curls upward; he kneels with folded hands before Rama, or tears open his chest to reveal Rama and Sita seated within his heart.

His worship turns entirely on bhakti. The same being who could uproot mountains chose the posture of a servant, and that paradox is the theological heart of his cult. Devotees invoke him against fear, ill fortune, and the affliction of Shani (Saturn), who in tradition granted Hanuman a boon of relief for his devotees. Akharas and wrestling grounds across India keep his image as patron of celibate strength and discipline. He is the bridge between the worshipper and Rama, never receiving devotion for himself alone.

Tuesday and Saturday are the days set aside for Hanuman, when his temples fill and the Chalisa is recited forty times or more. Hanuman Jayanti, marking his birth, falls on the full-moon day of Chaitra in much of North India, though Tamil and Telugu regions observe it in other months by their own reckonings. During the nine nights of Ramayana recitation and on Diwali eve in some traditions, his protection is invoked. Fasting on Tuesdays and offering sindoor, boondi, and a red cloth are common vows.

The stotra tradition around Hanuman is among the most recited in all of Hinduism. The Hanuman Chalisa, Tulsidas's forty-verse hymn, is chanted daily by millions. The Bajrang Baan and the Sankat Mochan Hanuman Ashtak, also attributed to the Tulsidas tradition, are turned to in distress and danger. The Hanuman Bahuk addresses bodily affliction and pain. For deeper liturgy devotees recite the entire Sundara Kanda and the Hanuman Ashtottara Shatanamavali of one hundred and eight names. Each composition in the collection below serves a defined devotional need.

No rite begins until he is first invoked. Ganesha (गणेश), the elephant-headed son of Shiva and Parvati, holds the office of Vighnaharta and Vighnakarta at once, the remover of obstacles and the one who places them where they are deserved. The well-known account in the Shiva Purana tells how Parvati formed him from the substance of her own body to guard her door, how Shiva severed his head in ignorance, and how the head of an elephant was fitted in its place, granting him primacy among the ganas.

Ganesha's narratives are gathered in the Ganesha Purana and the Mudgala Purana, the two Upapuranas devoted entirely to him, the latter expounding his eight incarnations. The Shiva Purana's Rudra Samhita gives the birth and beheading account. The Ganapati Atharvashirsha, an Upanishadic text of the Atharva tradition, identifies him with the supreme Brahman and the sacred syllable. He is also credited as the scribe who wrote the Mahabharata to Vyasa's dictation, a tradition recorded in the epic's opening Adi Parva.

Ganesha is portrayed with a single elephant head, a broken tusk held in one hand, and a rounded belly said to contain the cosmos. His several arms carry the noose (pasha) to draw devotees near, the goad (ankusha) to steer them, an axe (parashu) to cut attachment, and a bowl of modakas, the sweet he favours. The broken tusk recalls the tradition that he snapped it off to continue writing the Mahabharata. His mount is the mouse (mushika), and the trunk often curves toward a sweet.

He governs thresholds, both literal and figurative. Students invoke him before examinations, merchants before opening ledgers, builders before laying foundations, and households before weddings and journeys. The first syllable of his worship, the invocation Om Gam Ganapataye Namah, opens countless ceremonies. Because he both raises and clears obstacles, he is approached not merely for ease but for the wisdom to meet difficulty rightly. His association with Buddhi (intellect) and Siddhi (attainment), named in tradition as his consorts, frames him as the patron of learning.

Ganesha Chaturthi, on the fourth day of the bright fortnight of Bhadrapada, is his principal festival, observed with installed clay images, ten days of worship, and immersion on Anant Chaturdashi. Sankashti Chaturthi, the fourth day of each waning fortnight, and Vinayaka Chaturthi in the waxing fortnight are monthly observances marked by fasting until moonrise. Wednesday is associated with him in some traditions. Offerings of modaka, durva grass, and red flowers (especially hibiscus) accompany these vows across Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, and beyond.

Ganesha's stotra literature opens almost every devotional collection, fittingly. The Ganapati Atharvashirsha is recited as both hymn and Upanishad. Adi Shankaracharya's Ganesha Pancharatnam, a garland of five verses, is among the most loved. The Sankata Nashana Ganesha Stotra, drawn from the Narada Purana, is turned to in trouble, and the Ganesha Ashtottara Shatanamavali enumerates one hundred and eight names. The Vakratunda Mahakaya shloka opens worship as the universal invocation. Each text below answers a particular purpose, from daily worship to the clearing of grave difficulty.

On the field of Kurukshetra, with two armies arrayed and the warrior Arjuna sunk in despair, the charioteer set down the reins and spoke the Bhagavad Gita. That charioteer was Krishna (कृष्ण), the eighth avatar of Vishnu, and for much of the Hindu world he is not an avatar at all but Svayam Bhagavan, God in person and complete. The Bhagavata Purana opens his story with the prophecy that he would slay the tyrant Kamsa, his own uncle, and restore dharma to Mathura.

Krishna's life is told most fully in the tenth canto of the Bhagavata Purana, from his birth in Kamsa's prison to his childhood in Gokul and Vrindavan and his later kingship at Dwarka. The Mahabharata frames his role as statesman, kinsman, and divine guide, and within its Bhishma Parva sits the Bhagavad Gita, his eighteen-chapter teaching to Arjuna. The Harivamsha, an appendix to the epic, and the Vishnu Purana supply further detail of his early years and the lifting of Govardhana.

Krishna is depicted with a dark or blue complexion, the name itself meaning the dark one, standing in a graceful three-bent (tribhanga) pose with a flute (bansuri) raised to his lips. A peacock feather crowns his head, a yellow garment (pitambara) wraps his waist, and a garland of forest flowers (vaijayanti) hangs to his knees. As cowherd he is shown with cattle and gopis; as the Gita's teacher he stands in the chariot beside Arjuna; as the infant he reaches for stolen butter.

Two settings hold his devotion in tension and harmony. Vrindavan gives the Krishna of lila, of the flute and the raas dance with the gopis, whose love for Radha became, in the hands of the Gaudiya, Pushtimarg, and Nimbarka schools, the highest emblem of the soul's longing for God. Kurukshetra gives the Krishna of counsel, who teaches karma, bhakti, and jnana as paths to the divine. The boy who stole butter and the sage who steadied Arjuna are confessed to be one being.

Janmashtami, on the eighth day of the waning fortnight of Bhadrapada, marks his midnight birth and is kept with fasting, vigil, and the cradle rite at temples across India, most intensely at Mathura and Vrindavan. Radhashtami follows fifteen days later for Radha's appearance. Govardhan Puja, the day after Diwali, recalls his lifting of the hill, and Gita Jayanti in Margashirsha honours the giving of the Gita. Offerings of butter, milk sweets, tulsi leaves, and the fifty-six-item bhog (chappan bhog) attend these days.

Krishna's stotra tradition is vast and tender by turns. Adi Shankaracharya's Bhaja Govindam urges the mind toward devotion, and his Achyutashtakam and the Madhurashtakam, attributed to Vallabhacharya, dwell on Krishna's sweetness. The Bhagavata's Bhishma Stuti and the Gopis' songs are sung as concentrated praise, and the Govinda Damodara Stotra and Krishnashtakam are widely chanted. Many devotees recite the entire Bhagavad Gita or its dhyana verses daily. The collection below ranges from the playful to the philosophical, each suited to a distinct mood of devotion.

When the gods had been driven from heaven and no male deity could overcome the buffalo-demon, their massed anger took form as a woman radiant beyond the sun, and each god gave her his weapon. So the Devi Mahatmya describes the birth of Durga (दुर्गा), the warrior goddess whose name means the impassable, the fortress none can breach. She is Shakti, the active power of the divine, worshipped in the Shakta tradition as the supreme reality from which even the great gods derive their strength.

The foundational text of her worship is the Devi Mahatmya, also called the Durga Saptashati or Chandi Path, set within the Markandeya Purana across chapters eighty-one to ninety-three. Its seven hundred verses narrate her three great battles. The Devi Bhagavata Purana expands her theology as the Mahadevi, source of all. The Lalita Sahasranama, drawn from the Brahmanda Purana, praises her as Lalita Tripurasundari, while Adi Shankaracharya's Saundarya Lahari and Devi Aparadha Kshamapana Stotra belong to the same devotional stream.

Durga is shown riding a lion or tiger, her many arms each grasping a weapon gifted by the gods, the trishula (trident) of Shiva, the chakra of Vishnu, the vajra of Indra, the bow of Vayu, the conch, the sword, and the noose. Her face is serene even in battle. In her most celebrated form, Mahishasuramardini, she drives the trident into the buffalo-demon as he emerges from the severed body of the buffalo. She is golden-hued, ten-armed, and crowned, ferocity and composure held in one image.

The central myth is the slaying of Mahishasura, the buffalo-demon who had won a boon that no man or god could kill him and so was undone by a woman. Across the Devi Mahatmya's three episodes she also destroys Madhu and Kaitabha, then Shumbha and Nishumbha with their generals Chanda, Munda, and Raktabija, from whose blood she manifests Kali. These battles are read not only as history but as the soul's victory over ego, desire, and the forces that besiege dharma.

Her great season is Navaratri, the nine nights observed twice yearly, most grandly in the bright fortnight of Ashvin, culminating in Vijayadashami, the day of victory. In Bengal these days are Durga Puja, when clay images of the goddess and her children are installed, worshipped, and immersed. The ninth day, Mahanavami, and the eighth, Durgashtami, draw the most intense worship and the recitation of the full Saptashati. Offerings of red hibiscus, vermilion, and on certain days animal or symbolic sacrifice mark her rites.

Durga's stotra tradition centres on the recitation of the Devi Mahatmya itself, the Chandi Path, often chanted in full during Navaratri. Within and around it stand the Argala Stotra, Keelaka Stotra, and the Devi Kavacham, the protective armour recited before the main text. The Mahishasura Mardini Stotram, beginning Ayi Giri Nandini, is sung for its driving rhythm. Adi Shankaracharya's Devi Aparadha Kshamapana Stotra seeks forgiveness for faults in worship. Each composition below answers a need, from protection and victory to the seeking of the Mother's grace.
